title: True Crime Updates
dek: A round-up of the latest true crime stories from around the US
meta_description: True crime updates from Law & Crime
body_html:
A federal judge has rejected a bid by a January 6 pipe bomb suspect to dismiss his case using Trump's clemency order, as reported by Law & Crime. The suspect, who is a 2020 election denier, allegedly planted bombs near the Capitol before January 6. This development is significant because it highlights the ongoing impact of the January 6 insurrection and the attempts by some individuals to use presidential directives to evade accountability. According to Law & Crime, the judge observed that the suspect did not have 'pending indictments' at the time of the President's directive, and therefore the pardon does not apply to him.

The case against the suspect is ongoing, with the judge's ruling marking a significant setback for the defense. As reported by Law & Crime, the suspect was first charged in December 2025, months after the President's proclamation. This timeline is crucial in understanding the judge's decision, as it highlights the fact that the suspect did not have pending indictments at the time of the President's directive. The judge's ruling is a reminder that presidential pardons are not a blanket immunity for individuals facing criminal charges.

The use of presidential pardons and clemency orders has been a topic of controversy in recent years, with some arguing that they can be used to undermine the rule of law. In this case, the judge's ruling suggests that the pardon does not apply to the suspect, and that he will need to face the consequences of his actions. As reported by Law & Crime, the suspect's case is ongoing, and it will be important to watch how it develops in the coming weeks and months.
